speaker: interviewer
text: Today is November 30th, 1995. My name is Leslie Wilder. And I will be conducting an interview with Borys Eizenstein in Winnipeg, Canada. The interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Leslie Wilder, L-E-S-L-I-E W-I-L-D-E-R. Today is Thursday, November 30, 1995. I am conducting an interview with Borys Eizenstein in Winnipeg, Manitoba, Canada. Could I ask your name please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name? Borys Eizenstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's spelled B-O-R-Y-S E-I-Z--

speaker: interviewee
text: S-T-E-I-N. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 26th of June, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nickname Pesach-- Pesach.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pesach. And what country were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland. In which city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the city Korzec, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland. That's spelled K-O-R-C-U. Were you were raised in Korzec?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- my mother's from Korzec. And my father's from Selisht. They called in Polish, Ludwipol. And in Jewish they called him [INAUDIBLE] Selisht. And [INAUDIBLE] was a [INAUDIBLE] Selisht in [INAUDIBLE]. So they called him [INAUDIBLE] Selisht. And my-- when I-- I was born in Korzec. But my father and-- they got a home. They build a home in Selisht, Ludwipol. And then I was-- we were moved back to, after the [INAUDIBLE], I don't know when--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK

speaker: interviewee
text: --but they moved back to Selisht. I was raised in Selisht--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Until 1940. What do you remember about Selisht?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, lots of things. I was a very bad boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a very bad boy. An active boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about some of the things you did.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody want fish have to pay me $0.25.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you when you got into that little business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I think about eight, nine years old. And I used to have-- make shows in my garage, in our garage. I used to make shows and sell tickets. And-- and I used to acting. What else? And done with fishing. And my mother never let bring in the fish to the house. So we stole a frying pan. And another kid stole onions. And everybody stole something. And we, beside the river, we used to make our meals.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: And we come home dirty. And-- and we couldn't eat. Why? We got our food already. We didn't eat. So we used to get slaps, you know? And I got a dog. And a dog, my mother didn't like to keep him. So my friends kept him for me. And then I got pigeons. These didn't help my mother nothing about it. I raised up in the roof. They called them pigeons, were they-- for the male. You know, male pigeons, they were army pigeons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes

speaker: interviewee
text: So somehow we got them from the army. I don't know how. We-- from the army, we stole a few, and we raised them. They got [? crowns, ?] you know. And we raised them. I used to sell them to kids, you know. I was a businessman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do with your profits?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we spent, you know. We spent foolish, you know, for candies. We bought cigarettes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you smoke them at eight years old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. In the school [INAUDIBLE], we used to pick up the fingers we got in toilet [INAUDIBLE] to smoke. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me more about your school life when you weren't smoking in the-- in the back room.

speaker: interviewee
text: In school life, I was in a public school, not in Jewish school-- me and [INAUDIBLE], one kid, you know, what he [? didn't ?] his father, too. And they couldn't afford to go in Jewish school. So me and him, we went to public school that's in 4 kilometers from Selisht.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your father pass away?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was four years old. And they robbed my mother. My father was very rich. [CRYING] They-- To say in plain English, they robbed her. And the business man, she gave the money away to she trusted. She was very young. And she gave it away to the businessmen for interest. They never give you the money. They never give you interest. So she went to work for [INAUDIBLE]. He got a little money factory from clothing. You know, they make cloth for woman, there for men.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I used to come from school. 4 kilometers, I walked. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] [SOBS] My mother wasn't home. And I was hungry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was already 5 o'clock. My mother supposed to work 8 hours. I come in. And I knew on our-- I knew on our clock, you know, we got 5 o'clock. I went into the [INAUDIBLE]. And this clock was 3:00. He was moving the clock backwards. You know, [? cheating ?], robbing people. And you couldn't do nothing about it. We didn't have a judge. We didn't have nobody. The only thing, a rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: What could do the rabbi? And we got one chief police-- a sergeant major. I went to him. And he couldn't do nothing about it. He says those belong to the Jewish society. I couldn't fight him, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my family, my uncles-- I got four brothers, my dad. And I got my grandfather. I don't know why they didn't help us, not me, not my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a close relationship with your uncles and your grandfathers?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my-- very close, very close. And we very close. But they didn't-- this was just five zlotys a month to pay-- to come [? in the ?] Hebrew school to be together with the-- with the [? all ?] kids. [CRYING] [INTERPOSING VOICES] You understand? What kind of rotten society? And, you know? And we were two kids. But in Friday, the Polish government allow-- the rabbi used to come for two hours to teach us-- to teach us the-- the Torah, you know, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, how to pray.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: To prepare me for Bar-- for Bar Mitzvah. But the only thing in Bar Mitzvah, my grandfather bought me a horse, one for the army, you know, very good horse. And I took him for a ride. And I-- I give him lots of water. I mean, I didn't know all about it. And he blow up, he died. He killed himself. So that was my gift for Bar Mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large was the Jewish community in Ludwipol?

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot to check about it. But it was a large Jewish community.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they all attending your Bar Mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the only thing in Bar Mitzvah is they made a [INAUDIBLE] in a bottle of Vodka. My grandfather did it. And I remember the Bar Mitzvah, everything. Yeah. But they didn't take care of us. And my mother survived from rent. She was working, and we rented up a half house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who lived in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: There lived my uncles a few years. Then I threw out one uncle. I threw him out myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: You threw him out yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Through the windows, everything. He didn't pay. And we let in-- from the [INAUDIBLE], one family got their house burned. So they stay a year. Then the [INAUDIBLE] were staying. And in 1939, the Russians come in. So the secretary was a woman. She was like the mayor. In Russia, the Communist party with a Secretary, he becomes the Mayor or the provisional government, goes by Secretary. She was a woman with one child-- a girl. Her husband got killed in Finland. She rent a half house. And she pay all the utilities. And everything she supplies, food and everything. When the Russians come in, you know, everything was closed up. They took away everything we got. The only survivor was from the villages trading goods for food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so what happened? The chief police [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was Ukrainian. And he come to her house. And he told his-- my father was his father-- they went together in school. Somehow they were very close friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he knows. My father told him all about the [INAUDIBLE] business, you know. And he knows someone who find out who-- who robbed my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have to say it robbing. You know why? They robbed her. She was a woman. It was very bad, very bad. I'll never forgive them, the society from Ludwipol, what they did to my mom and to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he find out, about four families, where they were, got shoe-- shoe business. And he went with me. And he told them to give back money. They didn't have money, so he took from them leather. And we took lots of leather away from the three places. The rest I don't know. And he-- they brought it to us. And my mother used-- I used to go to the villagers and-- and, you know, and I used to trade in, you know, the skins to trade in for food, you know, potatoes, wood, things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in 1939?

speaker: interviewer
text: And to clarify, Mr. Eizenstein, you were the sole-- you and your mother were the sole immediate members of your family. You had no brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had no--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I just was the only one.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the only one.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you another. But our society, the good society, Jewish society from Ludwipol, we were having, you know-- behind the main street are-- are bungalow, beautiful home with a garage. So my neighbor was from the [INAUDIBLE]. He was-- you know, he-- you know, he got wounded in the First World War somehow. I come back from the school, and he built a terrace.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I come in to him. My mother was working. I come in to him. And I told him, why you're building a terrace? [INAUDIBLE] going bring in wood to her home? You're covering up our entry to her house from the market. You understand me? And he took three quarter. Hardly you can get through-- ourselves even. So how can a wagon get through to bring us wood? And he kicked me, and he says, you [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what a [NON-ENGLISH] is? A bastard. I says I got my [INAUDIBLE]. My father was your brother like. We lived together. I play with your kids. I come. So my uncle Yosel, he bought me a gun. I kept it in the chimney, loaded, eight bullets. I picked up the gun from there. And I shot him in the-- excuse me-- in the hip. So-- and I told the builders to run away.

speaker: interviewee
text: One was a gentile man, and one was a Jewish guy. They run away. And I tell them, you come back I'll kill you like dogs. And so then there come the chief police, come the sergeant. And I-- you know, they figure out they're going to arrest me, going to kill me, that and that. But anyways, and I told the whole story. And I-- he called up workers to dismiss the terrace. And he told him next time you touch him, if you kills you, you can't do nothing to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that incident?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's when the Russians were the government?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry. That's not right. No, that was Polish government. And Russian-- [LAUGHS] that was in I think '37, or '38--

speaker: interviewer
text: '37-- before.

speaker: interviewee
text: '38, Something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: '37 or '38. I was eight years old. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When did you start hearing about the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I start hearing about the war? OK. We finished with Selisht? OK. 1939, when they moved in, they took me. All the kids went to Russian school. You have to go to school. So we went to public school. And in 1940, the-- the lady lived in our place, the-- the mayor. And she told me why shouldn't I go and enjoy

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Russian? What do you call them?

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- the youth movement?

speaker: interviewee
text: The youth movement. So when they come to the youth movement, they didn't accept me on account I was a Revisionist. I was Jabotinsky's rightist group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you were you a member of the Jabotinsky group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, [INAUDIBLE]. I was the leader from the youths.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the leader. Oh, tell me about that. Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got very strong. If you knew maybe a [INAUDIBLE] here-- he was here, [INAUDIBLE] here died in Winnipeg-- he was a [INAUDIBLE]. We used to fight every Saturday in-- you know, with the tallis. They used to fight with the [INAUDIBLE], you know? [INAUDIBLE] with the-- with the right [INAUDIBLE]. We got our own uniform. And we got guns. We were trained to kill and shoot, exercise. And in 19-- I don't remember what year was Jabotinsky in-- youth, was a conference in [INAUDIBLE]. We went there. We walked, I mean, a few days.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you went from your city?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember it was, but it was lots, lots of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there young men and young women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Young women and young men. We got our own club. We got everything I own-- you know, pinball. What do you call them? Pinball, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Pinball, billiard tables.

speaker: interviewee
text: Machines and billiards, yeah. We got our own club. You know, we very discipline. Speeches used to come in-- speakers. And we were very organized, very organized.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were trained for self-defense. And the police didn't-- why? I went to get in school with the chief police son. I was sleeping one day in his place and one day in my place. They got a huge farm. In time from the holidays, for the two months I used to go to their farm, about 20 kilometer or 30 kilometer. Huge-- not farm, what do you call it? Ranch. Ranch?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ranch. Horses, you know. You name it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a close relationship with some of the non-Jewish members.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So I used to go-- they used to give me guns freely. I used to go into police station to get guns. And I used to bring them back, yeah, with bullets, live, everything.

words.text: Yeah. So I used to go--

speaker: interviewer
text: So when-- so they didn't accept me. And the communist youths from the kibbutzim with [INAUDIBLE], you know, from the [INAUDIBLE], they didn't accept me for [INAUDIBLE] on account I was a rightist. But she-- she made it for me, papers. And then they send away eight youths to academy-- engineering, like here, Red River College. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was a camp from the Polish-- the Polish-Russian border, was a huge camp for officers, you know, the most modern. Even today, you can see it, the way it was done then to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe it for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe it for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Describe it? It was like a pensione, barracks, but little barracks, with say for 10, 12 people, two in a room and beautiful, the bedding, the beds. And it was not one on the top of another, just two in each room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did you room with?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little gentile guy. Yeah, from-- I don't know from where we were. 180 students. And down the hallway was a restaurant. Like, today, I didn't see even a famous [INAUDIBLE] what I show in television. I still don't see it, the beauty, decoration, with chocolates, flowers every morning. We got four meals a day. And the uniforms, that's-- not-- the richness is indescribable. And we were training a half day engineering and a half day army.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the things that you did--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the things you did in your training?

speaker: interviewee
text: I choose training-- I was choosing engineering for building, building. In Russia, it's not like here. In Russia you start-- they-- they made us to dig foundations. You understand?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we have to take the 2 by 4 and make up the forms from wood. We have to do ourselves. That's what I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then the bricks, we have to make our own bricks. There was ovens. And we have to know the temperature for so long to-- to-- to bake them, you know, to make them ready, to get red. You understand? Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we-- they taught us how to put piles and to build the buildings. And then start the war in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many Jewish students at--

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight. Eight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eight students.

speaker: interviewee
text: One just got killed. He went back home. [INAUDIBLE], his brother's still here. He got three brothers here in Winnipeg.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were eight Jewish students out of a total of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The rest were all gentiles.

speaker: interviewer
text: The rest were all-- and how did you get along with the gentile students?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's unbelievable. It's no racial discrimination in my life. Everybody knows I'm Jewish, and everybody knew, and everybody, went my own name. No matter I'm Poland or in Russia, I never got the feelings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was treated even better.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to observe the holidays and Shabbat at these training academy?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you another story. [INAUDIBLE] want it-- you want it [INAUDIBLE]. This one, when I was in school, no. But I want to bring in, you know, to tell you from the school. You want me to tell you about the school before the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Certainly. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I become-- after three months, I become instructor for sport. So they took me away from the headquarters from the students to the headquarters from the staff from the school. You understand me? What the general was, the instructors, you know, the teachers, they got their own headquarters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: You understand? And they got a separate kitchen, better-- still better treated, you know. They didn't mix up with students. So I went in with them and live with them in headquarters.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this way I met the whole, all big shots. And I was already having a free pass. I could go anywhere I want when I want. Students, you know, got so much leave so, you know, in a week, leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 220 kilometers to town. Our camp was in a bush beside a station. This was the border. About 3, 4 kilometers was the Russian border. And we got a train station there. And-- and [? then ?] was a village, Polish village.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I met there a Polish family. And they accept me so closely, like a son. And they were on account their son. And they got a daughter. They got sons.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took me in the, like, [INAUDIBLE], they've got no place to go. On Sunday, or when I got the evenings off, I went with them. And I show, you know-- I wanted badly my mom to see me. So the instructor what I become very friendly, the instructor from sport, you know. And he was an invalid from Finland war, the war from Finland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very wounded, very crippled. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was with his family there living. And we become like-- like father son, son to father. And one day he says to me, he's going to Kiev-- he was from Kiev-- to see his parents if I want to join him.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, sure I want to join. I want to see Kiev. I never saw in my life Kiev. But I told him, you know what? I want to bring my mom.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm the only kid. And my mom, they called him [INAUDIBLE] that's Ukraina. You know, that's not Russian Ukraine. That's Polish Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he says, Borys, no problem. I go with you to Kiev the city. I want to get you a pass and the bring your mom. And the government's going pay for it. You understand me? So we went in to army supermarket. Why? He's in the army. And he bought so-- for the army, it's cheap there, you know. Hardly you have to pay. He got gifts. And then he took me to the city hall. And who was there? The Secretary was Khrushchev. He was the Secretary from the party. He was like the boss, the mayor like, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took me to him. I mean, he filled me out the application, mine lieutenant, you know, from the sport, from the school. He filled out application. I was shaking. And I didn't know in mine age, you know, this. And he wasn't famous. He was just a [INAUDIBLE]. Khrushchev then was like everybody else. And I went into the office. They give me a chair. And he asked me questions. And he told me if I'm a Komsomolets, and I says to him I'm not a Komsomolets. And-- and the only thing good, I told him the truth, you know, of what's going on, why.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he recommend me to be a Komsomol-- he signed for me to be a Komsomolets. Anyways, he give me the papers for my mom, the visa. And they send my mom to meet a [? comrade. ?] This Polish family took him for the two weeks, my mom to their house. And what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was still 1941.

speaker: interviewee
text: '41, in spring. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he got so appointed there. So I ask him-- [? we ?] throwing up lots of food. So I told him how about they give me a few pigs and turkeys, things like that if we put in fence, if we going give the food what we're throwing out to have a farm. And they give it to me. We made a big farm. And then they produced, you know. And we used to have our own-- our own pigs, our own poultry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was just you and your mother in that area?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. That's-- my mother went away after two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: After two weeks, your mother went back to Ludwipol.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah. And then start the war. And that's was-- we didn't know nothing. I mean, we knew the war goes on. And that's why they train us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1941, when the war broke out. They took us away from the engineering, you know. All the students went live training, defense.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they tell you about the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't-- they didn't tell us nothing. They didn't tell us nothing at all. You know, they told us the war goes on. But then when the planes-- after three days when the war come out, the planes come to bomb us. But they didn't hit us. They hit just the station. And the machine guns, what they got it to defense, was, you know, like a toy. And as we grabbed everything, we took to our school to defend ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: One day, we wake up in the morning. Nobody is there, all run away. We slept in. Who slept in? Was maybe two or three dozen kids slept in. You understand. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: We walk in, nobody was, not the generals, not the staff. Nobody was, not the waitress, no cooks, nothing. Everything was deserted.

speaker: interviewer
text: We got lost. What I was, 13 years old, 14. How old are we? We didn't know what to do. To run home? So one from the [INAUDIBLE] says he want to go home back with another gentile. So we give him letters. We told him not to go to Germans. They told us that the Germans killing everybody, not just Jewish. And we didn't know what to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyways, we didn't have enough weapons. But in the magazines was locked steel doors. So I took a weapon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the magazines were the storage areas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I took a weapon. And I shot in the-- in the lock. And we opened up the doors. And we took weapon. And I told weapon and food. The guys want to grab money. There was, you know, you name it. There was lots of food. I says, just grab food, everything you can, and weapons. And we run in the bush. In the bush, we find just the sergeant. He couldn't run. He was an Invalid.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was from the Finish war.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was with his family there hiding. But all generals, the whole-- the whole shoodle-boodle, everybody was on his own. But we left to save a dozen, dozen and-a-half kids. And to us, he was like a father. And he says, Borys, the only thing is self-defense, partisan. You go to partisan.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you formed a partisan unit.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went in the bushes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who-- who exactly went in the bushes with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: But we can say about 18 or 19 kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what ages were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same ages.

speaker: interviewer
text: Same ages, 13

speaker: interviewee
text: Same ages. We didn't want to go back home. So in the bush we went in to kill to survive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So the villagers, we went, they didn't want to give us food. We killed, and we took what we have to take.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you kill?

speaker: interviewee
text: We kill everybody. If not they kill us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody, the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. The people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Not every-- everybody or everything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I explain you what was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked into homes. There was lots of volksdeutsches. Lots of them got radios, what they give the Germans all information. You understand? We got information from the partisans, from the wounded soldiers, from the Russian army when they couldn't move.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we went free, you know. I mean, me or you, you understand? I don't want to go into it, you know. It was me or you, you or me, right? And the Germans were already all over. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You understand? The Germans was all over, fully equipped. We didn't. The only thing we got  is rifles and guns. A few grenades. The Germans got tanks, machines. And there in these areas in general in Poland, Ukraine, there were lots of volksdeutsche. You understand me? Hitler prepared them before the war. They brought in settlers. He was so smart, Hitler. Even in our province, he sent in volksdeutsche, settlers. They bought up land, and they settled down. And they all got stations. [INAUDIBLE] how you took it, even the Russian army, what was at [INAUDIBLE], if you heard about it. Or, you don't know about it? We'll pick that up on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
